refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall establish and maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication with p95 latency ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe remote operation by the tele-operator.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks specificity on measurement boundaries (end-to-end vs. component), network assumptions, and safety context. Refinement clarifies that latency is measured from operator input to equipment response, bounded by network conditions, and directly supports safe remote operation—a high-criticality safety function.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events at operator input, network ingress/egress, and equipment response; collect p95 latency under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); validate against 150 ms threshold under worst-case operational scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands over the network link within agreed latency budget.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode operator commands at the vehicle with minimal reception-to-actuation latency.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health to enable operator awareness and safe operation decisions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a dedicated low-latency teleoperation protocol with priority queuing, adaptive compression, and direct network path optimization.
DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining edge computing (local command buffering and prediction) with optimized network transport and vehicle-side command execution prioritization.
DP-C: Use a standard industrial teleoperation middleware (e.g., ROS 2 with DDS) with network QoS tuning and latency monitoring.
DP-D: Implement a multi-path redundant network with latency-aware routing and failover to maintain p95 ≤ 150 ms under network degradation.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-B: Deploy a hybrid architecture combining edge computing (local command buffering and prediction) with optimized network transport and vehicle-side command execution prioritization.

potential_sources: Edge computing placement couples with network topology and site infrastructure availability.
Command prediction logic couples with vehicle dynamics model and operator behavior assumptions.
Prioritization scheme couples with safety-critical command classification (FR-NGHE-008.4 latency reporting).
coupling_check.notes: DP-B exhibits moderate coupling but is manageable through clear interface contracts (Tasking protocol, SafetyAlert signaling). Coupling is primarily with network infrastructure (external) and vehicle command execution (internal to Vehicle module), both of which are within scope of NGHE solution design.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Edge computing infrastructure (local gateway/MEC node) is available at the worksite; Risk: If unavailable, fallback to DP-C (standard middleware) required.
Assumption: Vehicle dynamics model is sufficiently accurate for command prediction; Risk: Prediction errors could degrade safety; mitigation: prediction used only for non-safety-critical commands.

Assumption: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss); Risk: Transient network degradation could exceed p95 threshold; mitigation: DP-D (multi-path routing) as contingency.
Assumption: Operator latency perception threshold is ≤ 150 ms for safe remote operation; Risk: Human factors validation needed; mitigation: conduct operator acceptance testing.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between 'edge computing' and 'vehicle-side' command execution in DP-B. Specify whether edge computing is co-located with Teleoperation module or deployed as a separate infrastructure component. This affects IM assignment and ownership.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication path including operator command acquisition, network transport optimization, vehicle-side command reception/decoding, and latency monitoring. Includes edge computing logic for command buffering and prediction, network protocol implementation, and latency telemetry collection.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs operator commands (decoded and prioritized) to Vehicle module via Tasking interface for execution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives vehicle state telemetry (position, orientation, sensor data) from Vehicle module to support latency monitoring and operator feedback.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link interface for bidirectional communication with remote operator console and edge computing infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module outputs latency health alerts and safety-critical status via SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services module.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation module is the natural owner of latency-critical teleoperation logic. All core functions (command acquisition, network optimization, vehicle-side reception, latency monitoring) are cohesive and belong within this module. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert).

Vehicle module owns command execution but not teleoperation communication. Assigning latency management to Vehicle would scatter teleoperation logic across modules and couple Vehicle to network transport details. Not recommended.

Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerting, not teleoperation communication. Assigning latency management to Safety Services would violate separation of concerns. Safety Services should consume latency health alerts from Teleoperation, not own the latency mechanism.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. Assigning latency management at this level would bypass module-level ownership and create diffusion across all sub-modules. Not appropriate for detailed implementation.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the correct owner of CR-NGHE-008 (latency requirement). It owns the end-to-end communication path, has clear external interfaces (Tasking to Vehicle, SafetyAlert to Safety Services, Network Link to infrastructure), and exhibits high cohesion with minimal diffusion.
containment_risks: Risk: Edge computing placement (local gateway vs. cloud) is not yet decided; if edge computing is deployed as separate infrastructure, ownership boundary may need clarification (Teleoperation owns logic, but infrastructure team owns deployment).
Risk: Network Link interface abstraction may hide latency variability; if Network Link does not expose latency metrics, Teleoperation cannot monitor p95 latency effectively.
Risk: Vehicle module command execution latency is not explicitly owned; if Vehicle-side latency is significant, Teleoperation module cannot fully control end-to-end latency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Ensure Network Link interface exposes latency metrics (e.g., round-trip time, packet loss) to Teleoperation module. Define clear latency budget allocation: operator input → Teleoperation (X ms), Network Link (Y ms), Vehicle reception/execution (Z ms), where X + Y + Z ≤ 150 ms. Clarify whether Vehicle module owns command execution latency or if Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency including Vehicle-side delays.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-B (hybrid edge computing + optimized transport + vehicle-side prioritization) directly addresses the refined FR (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms). The DP is solution-agnostic (does not prescribe specific technologies) and is decomposable into sub-DPs for each sub-FR. No decoupling or independence violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Edge computing placement: If edge computing is deployed as external infrastructure (not owned by Teleoperation module), ownership and interface contracts must be explicit. Recommend: Teleoperation module owns the logic; infrastructure team owns deployment.
Network Link abstraction: If Network Link interface does not expose latency metrics, Teleoperation cannot monitor p95 latency. Recommend: Extend Network Link interface to include latency telemetry.

Vehicle-side latency: If Vehicle module command execution latency is not controlled, Teleoperation cannot guarantee end-to-end latency. Recommend: Define latency budget allocation and ensure Vehicle module meets its allocation.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module is the correct owner with high containment (95/100) and low diffusion (5/100). External interfaces are well-defined (Tasking, Telemetry, Network Link, SafetyAlert). No evidence of scattered ownership or cross-module dependencies.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible: instrument teleoperation path with timestamped events, measure p95 latency under agreed network conditions, validate against 150 ms threshold. Teleoperation module owns the measurement points and can collect end-to-end latency data.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module is responsible for end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms. Sub-allocations to sub-FRs (operator input latency, network transport latency, vehicle reception latency, latency monitoring) are defined and traceable to sub-DPs.

recommended_revisions: Extend Network Link interface specification to include latency metrics (round-trip time, packet loss, jitter) to enable Teleoperation module to monitor and report p95 latency.

Define explicit latency budget allocation: operator input → Teleoperation (X ms), Network Link (Y ms), Vehicle reception/execution (Z ms), where X + Y + Z ≤ 150 ms. Ensure Vehicle module is allocated a latency budget and owns command execution latency.

Clarify edge computing placement and ownership: if edge computing is deployed as external infrastructure, define interface contracts and responsibility boundaries between Teleoperation module and infrastructure team.
Conduct human factors validation to confirm that operator latency perception threshold is ≤ 150 ms for safe remote operation under representative worksite conditions.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-formed, the selected DP is sound and decomposable, and the assigned IM (Teleoperation) is correct with high containment and low diffusion. However, the FR is not yet atomic: it comprises four sub-FRs (operator input latency, network transport latency, vehicle reception latency, latency monitoring) that require further decomposition. Proceeding to the next level will refine each sub-FR, select corresponding sub-DPs, and assign ownership to sub-modules or components within Teleoperation. Gate review identified three critical revisions (Network Link interface, latency budget allocation, edge computing placement) that should be addressed during sub-level decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire operator commands with minimal input-to-transmission latency (target: ≤ 30 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit operator commands over the network link within agreed latency budget (target: ≤ 80 ms for p95 under agreed network conditions).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The teleoperation system shall receive and decode operator commands at the vehicle with minimal reception-to-actuation latency (target: ≤ 40 ms).

FR-NGHE-008.4: The teleoperation system shall monitor and report latency health to enable operator awareness and safe operation decisions (target: latency metrics available to operator console and Safety Services with ≤ 10 ms reporting latency).

assumptions: Edge computing infrastructure (local gateway/MEC node) is available at the worksite.
Vehicle dynamics model is sufficiently accurate for command prediction (used only for non-safety-critical commands).
Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss).
Operator latency perception threshold is ≤ 150 ms for safe remote operation.
Network Link interface can be extended to expose latency metrics.
Vehicle module will own and meet a latency budget allocation for command execution.
open_risks: Edge computing placement not yet decided; if unavailable, fallback to standard middleware (DP-C) required.
Network Link interface may not expose latency metrics; if not, Teleoperation cannot monitor p95 latency effectively.
Vehicle-side latency not yet allocated; if Vehicle module does not own command execution latency, end-to-end latency cannot be guaranteed.
Operator latency perception threshold not yet validated; human factors testing required.
Transient network degradation could exceed p95 threshold; multi-path routing (DP-D) may be needed as contingency.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification including latency metrics (round-trip time, packet loss, jitter).
Latency budget allocation: operator input (X ms), network transport (Y ms), vehicle reception (Z ms), where X + Y + Z ≤ 150 ms.
Edge computing placement decision and infrastructure availability assessment.
Vehicle module latency budget allocation and command execution latency specification.
Human factors validation: operator latency perception threshold under representative worksite conditions.
Network condition profile: agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss, and worst-case scenarios.
